coredata.dc:title: Controlled delivery of dopamine hydrochloride using surface modified carbon dots for neuro diseases
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, M. Shahnawaz
Pandey, Sunil
Talib, Abou
Bhaisare, Mukesh Lavkush
Wu, Hui-Fen
coredata.dc:description: Delivery of therapeutic agents using water-soluble, highly biocompatible Carbon dots (C-dots) is an efficient strategy to control drug release under physiological milieu. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), the most important inotropic vasopressor agent used in neurological diseases. In our study DA is anchored to water-soluble carbon dots for controlled release under mimicked in vitro physiological conditions. The tenure of the DA release at pH 7.4 was greatly extended to 60h for C-dots–DA, in comparison with the control DA alone. The statistical calculation was used to comprehend the release pattern of the DA, which exhibited the pattern of Hixson–Crowell model of release. In order to understand the impact of the C-dots–DA conjugate under physiological conditions, Neuro 2A cells were taken under consideration. The conjugate C-dots–DA was found to be biocompatible against Neuro 2A cells. The survival rate was found to be 74% at maximum concentration of 9μgmL−1. In vivo toxicity was studied using thin section of tissues after staining with Hematoxyline and Eosin Yellow (H&E). As per microscopic observations, conjugates did not inflict any anatomical distortions or hostile effects on tissues. Body weight of mice was also taken into consideration after injecting 20μgmL−1 of nano-conjugates via tail vein. The impact of nano-conjugate on body weight was found to be negligible after 45 days of observation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine hydrochloride (DA)
C-dots
Drug delivery
Hixson–Crowell
Neuro 2A cells

Culturing Neuro 2A cells and interaction with DA
Drug release and kinetics of DA
Biocompatibility of C-dots–DA conjugates in Neuro 2A cells
Fluorescence microscopy of Neuro 2A cells with DA and C-dots–DA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of C-dots (a) UV–vis spectra of C-dots (black), DA (green), C-dots–DA (blue). (b) Showing the fluorescence of C-dots (red), C-dots-cyst Hyd (black), C-dots–DA (green). (c) TEM image of purified C-dots (d) XRD of C-dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)

(a) RAMAN of C-dots. (b) Zeta potential of C-dots, C-dots with cystamine hydrochloride and C-dots with cystamine hydrochloride and conjugate with dopamine hydrochloride. (c) FTIR spectra of C-dots showing their typical functionalization pattern with cystamine hydrochloride and dopamine hydrochloride
Biocompatibility of normal cells after injecting different conc of DA and C-dots–DA.
Percentage release of C-dots–DA and Dopamine with respect to time in PBS at pH 7.4.
Epifluorescent microscopic images normal cells treated by PBS, DA, and C-dots–DA.
Tissue section (stained by Haemotoxolin and Eosin) of brain, heart, liver, kidney and spleen were treated by DA and C-dots–DA.
Showing the percentage body weight of mice during the 45 days after injecting DA and C-dots–DA.
Effect of different concentration of C-dots on cell viability.

head.ce:title: Controlled delivery of dopamine hydrochloride using surface modified carbon dots for neuro diseases

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan. Tel.: +886 7 5252000 3955; fax: +886 7 5253909.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are first authors with equal contribution.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Delivery of therapeutic agents using water-soluble, highly biocompatible Carbon dots (C-dots) is an efficient strategy to control drug release under physiological milieu. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), the most important inotropic vasopressor agent used in neurological diseases. In our study DA is anchored to water-soluble carbon dots for controlled release under mimicked in vitro physiological conditions. The tenure of the DA release at pH 7.4 was greatly extended to 60h for C-dots–DA, in comparison with the control DA alone. The statistical calculation was used to comprehend the release pattern of the DA, which exhibited the pattern of Hixson–Crowell model of release. In order to understand the impact of the C-dots–DA conjugate under physiological conditions, Neuro 2A cells were taken under consideration. The conjugate C-dots–DA was found to be biocompatible against Neuro 2A cells. The survival rate was found to be 74% at maximum concentration of 9μgmL−1. In vivo toxicity was studied using thin section of tissues after staining with Hematoxyline and Eosin Yellow (H&E). As per microscopic observations, conjugates did not inflict any anatomical distortions or hostile effects on tissues. Body weight of mice was also taken into consideration after injecting 20μgmL−1 of nano-conjugates via tail vein. The impact of nano-conjugate on body weight was found to be negligible after 45 days of observation.

ce:section.ce:para: Transporting the active pharmaceutical agents to the specific target requires smart strategies and safer delivery systems, in order to optimize the efficacy of the drug and its interaction with the targets. With the advancements in medical nanotechnologies especially the development of nanomaterials, the drug delivery profile in terms of absorption, distribution and elimination of the drugs from the human body can be improved by using the approaches of drug–nanomaterial conjugates [1,2]. Water-soluble nanomaterials are the most popular and effective drug delivery methods due to their high capability to circulate in the biological fluids and exhibit long sustained drug release during the course of treatment. Due to their excellent biocompatibility and facile synthesis protocols, C-dots can be widely used in biomedical applications, including drug delivery and cellular imaging [1]. However, it is extremely difficult to deliver and sustain the drug in human brains, due to the blood-brain barrier, which can highly restrict the drugs such as foreign peptides, chemicals, and proteins from getting in to the brain [2]. In recent years, to overcome this hurdle, C-dots attracted the attention from scientists due to their multifaceted applicability; like in biological imaging and labeling, photo-catalysis, as a matrix, in sensor design, etc. [3–8]. C-dots have also been applied in determining the critical micelle concentration of various surfactants [9] and as a matrix for detection of low molecular weight of compounds using matrix assisted laser desorption/ionization-mass spectrometry (MALDI-MS) [10].

In contrast to C-dots, semiconductor quantum dots such as CdS, CdSe, CdTe quantum dots are used for bioimaging, but impart toxicity due to Cd+ release [11,12]. The toxicological effects of quantum dots may be attributed to the free radical generation [13]. These heavy semiconductor metals are toxic to important human organs, such as liver and kidney because they can easily penetrate to these organs [14]. For a competent drug delivery system, intense functionalization strategies to attach neuro-drugs with carrier are required [15,16]. Due to the inherent surface decoration with COOH, OH and NH2 functional groups, a variety of chemotherapeutic agents can be anchored to the surface of C-dots. Additionally, the functionalized C-dots, under physiological conditions, are not only photochemically stable but also exhibit excellent physiochemical stability [17]. Hence, designing sophisticated nanoscale vehicles that can carry a bulk of drug to the specific drugs are desperately solicited.

In chemotherapy, the problem arises when the drug disperses before reaching to the target, leading to less permeation as well as off-target toxicity [18]. Major attention has been paid in controlling the shape and size of nanoparticles, so that they can carry drugs inside the fine blood capillaries [19,20]. Over the past few years, sustained release of neurotransmitters for dopamine has received major attention. The C-dots, due to their porous nature, have shown high drug loading capacities as well as excellent drug delivery properties [15,16]. The possibility of C-dots as a drug delivery vehicle to carry neuro-therapeutic agents is explored in the present study. This study is concerned with comprehending the in vitro release kinetics and attachment chemistry of the C-dots to DA. The release pattern of DA was found to follow Hixson–Crowell model, which are considered to be ideal for injectables.

In the present work, we have employed C-dots for effective drug delivery and analyzing its toxic effects, both in vitro and in vivo. Anchored DA to C-dots showed sustained release over 60h than the bare DA (control). Both in vivo and in vitro toxicological studies have confirmed that the C-dots can be used as highly effective and biocompatible drug delivery systems.

Acetonitrile (ACN), anhydrous citric acid and chloroform were purchased from J.T. Baker, USA. Dopamine hydrochloride and 3-(4,5 dimethyl-thiazol-2-ul-2,5-diphenly tetrazolium bromide were purchased from Sigma, USA. 96 wells and T-flasks were bought from Nune, Thermo Scientific, USA. Dulbecco's Eagle's medium was bought from Thermo Scientific, USA (DMEM, HyClone-containing 4mM/L L-glutamine, 4500mg/L glucose), fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Gibco, USA. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS), Trypsin-EDTA solution (17,000U/L Trypsin mixed with 0.2gL−1 EDTA) and pen-strep solution (10,000U/L penicillin mixed with 10mg/mL streptomycin) were bought from Lonza, Belgium. C-dots crystallinity was analyzed by XRD (Philip, The Netherlands). Whereas, Raman spectrometer with laser wavelength 760nm was used. The water used for all experiments including the cleaning of glass wares were from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

Transmission Electron Microscope (JEOL, Japan) was used for the TEM images of C-dots. Carbon coated grids were drop casted using dilute solution of C-dots for the sample analysis. Surface passivation of C-dots was studied using Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA), after drop coating C-dots solution on KBr pellets. The optical properties of C-dots were recorded by double beam UV–VIS spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA) between 200 and 550nm. Same solution of C-dots was studied using fluorescence spectroscope (Hitachi, F-2700, Japan) at various excitation wavelengths (λ ext =200, 250, 300, 350nm). The microtomy of mice organs were executed by a Hestion ERM 3000 semi-automatic Microtome (Australia).

C-dots were synthesized in deionized (DI) water, in which 10mg of citric acid was heated in a microwave for 2min at 750watt, then equal volume of 1M NaOH and ethanol (3mL) were added to the reaction mixture (1:1 ratio) and further microwave treatment followed by 1min stirring to achieve a homogeneous solution. The resulting solution was transferred in a pre-activated dialysis bag (MW cut off 12–14kD) of pore size 2–6nm. To obtain nano-sized C-dots, they were dialyzed against nanopure water for 24h; then, a yellow bright solution of pure C-dots was obtained which was used for all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attachment of DA on C-dots (preparation of the nano-conjugate)
ce:section.ce:para: 15mg of cystamine hydrochloride was dissolved in 5mL of DI water (1500ppm). 40μL from this stock solution was added to the 1mL of pure C-dots and the total volume was adjusted to 3mL with water. After stirring the reaction mixture for 3h, 1mL of DA solution (0.02mg/mL) was added in the C-dots solution and further stirred at room temp for 3h and maintain the concentration at 25ppm.

3mL of the C-dots–DA conjugate was dialyzed against nanopure water. The conjugation efficiency of DA to C-dots was determined by the UV–vis spectroscopy, at 280nm wavelength. The DA drug loading efficiency (DLE) was measured by the following equation.
DLE =
Theoretical
amount
of
drug
loaded − Free
drug
Theoretical
amount
of
drug
loaded
× 100

7mL of either DA or C-dots–DA solutions were transferred in polycarbonate dialysis membrane (10kD, Spectrum Labs, USA) add 90mL Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) solution pH 7.4 was added. Samples were gently agitated in an incubator at 37̊C. To obtain a calibration curve for drug release profile, 3mL of eluent was procured at the regular interval. Percent cumulative drug release (% CR) was determined by using the following formula:
(2)
%
C R =
C
0 − f
C o
× 100
where C
o and C
f are the final concentration of the DA.

In vitro cytotoxicity of C-dots, DA, and C-dots–DA conjugates were investigated by using MTT assay. MTT assay is based on the mitochondrial oxidoreductase enzyme, which converts pale yellow MTT dye to the violet crystals of formazan. Cells were seeded (5×106 cellsmL−1) in 96 microtiter plates (3596 cell culture microtiter plate corning star corp-Germany), incubated at 5% CO2 at 37°C for 24h. C-dots, DA, C-dots–DA was added in the microtiter plate by replacing the medium and further incubating for 48h. After which, these solutions were subjected to MTT dye (200μgmL−l) and incubated for 2.5h at 28°C to start the formation of formazan. The cell medium was again replaced with DMSO then vortexed slowly for a few minutes, in order to dissolve the formazan crystals. The final solution was transferred in a microtiter plate for UV–vis analysis at 570nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Culturing Neuro 2A cells and interaction with DA
ce:section.ce:para: The Neuro 2A cells were grown in DMEM under humid conditions in CO2 incubator at 37°C for 24h. After ensuring the high confluency in growth (<80%), the cells were trypsinized using PBS solution. Neuro 2A cells (1×104 cellsmL−1) were seeded into 96 wells plates in 200μL of DMEM medium and were further incubated for 24h. After that, 20μL of DA and C-dots–DA were mixed with the cells to assess their interaction and hostile effects on the Neuro 2A cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug release and kinetics of DA
ce:section.ce:para: 10mL of DA and C-dots–DA conjugate sealed in a dialysis bag (MW cut off 12–14kD, pore size 2–4nm) were put in two beakers of 100mL of PBS solution (pH 7.4) at 37°C, under slow stirring. From each beaker, 3mL of samples were collected after 15min, 6h, 12h, and two days. 3mL of fresh PBS solution was added immediately after removing the samples for analysis. The concentration of both drug and the nano-conjugate were determined by using standard calibration curve at the wavelength of 280nm [21].

Under the influence of UV light (λ =365nm), the yellow color of C-dots was changed to the bright blue colored solution. This change in color is due to the presence of energy traps or quantum confinement of electron, sequestered within C-dots [22,23]. The absorption of C-dots appeared at 260–450nm in the UV–vis spectrum. A sharp peak at 276nm (Fig. 1(a)) was observed because of n →π* transition of electron linked with carbonyl groups [24]. The dual conjugative effects are accountable for this excitation. The first one is in nanometer scale sp2 clusters and the second one is from a chromophore or colored groups like CC, CC, CO bonds. On C-dots attachment with the cystamine hydrochloride, the sharp peak of C-dots is shifted from 276nm to 238nm followed by decrease in absorbance. Further conjugation of DA with C-dots results in blue shift of signature peak of C-dots to 236nm and characteristic appearance of DA peak, exhibited at 280nm [14].

In Fig. 1(b), the fluorescence studies of purified C-dots were used and excitation-based emission spectra of the C-dots [4,9,16] were recorded at 450nm, when excited at wavelength 425nm. C-dots attachment with cystamine hydrochloride, the peak intensity for C-dots decreased but there is no considerable change in number of peaks and their shapes. To determine the size of nanoparticles, actual diameter of each nanoparticle from the sample of more than 40 nanoparticles was measured from the TEM images. Fig. 1(c) displayed spherical shaped C-dots ranging from 5–10nm in diameter. Smaller size of C-dots among the carbon-based nanomaterial facilitates one to one interaction with the analyte. XRD pattern (Fig. 1(d)) of C-dots shows partial crystalline nature of nanomaterial with a broad peak at 2θ =43.12° which is attributed to (101) diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon. The peak at 26.17° shows interlayer spacing of around 3.67Å which is slightly more than that between (002) planes in bulk graphite, i.e. 3.44Å [25]. This augmentation in spacing is due to the oxygen-functionalized groups on the exterior of C-dots. Because of crystalline nature of C-dots, it can assemble in an ordered arrangement for efficient interaction and transmittance of laser energy, which helps in soft ionization of the analytes. The Raman spectra of C-dots showed (Fig. 2(a)) two peaks at around 1350cm−1 and 1540cm−1, which can be assigned as the D-band (sp3) and G-band (sp2). High intensity of D band (distortion band or defect band) is due to the presence of defects in the configuration of C-dots. The presence of two bands shows the amorphous nature of C-dots. These defects cause the fluorescence in the C-dots. The intensity of the G-band was found to be less prominent; this indicates the presence of less sp2 carbons. Because of these defects, C-dots can assemble as ultra-small oxygenated graphene oxide sheets; forming sp2–sp3 oxygen matrix and leading to electron hole pair formation, which gives color under UV light [26–29].

The occurrences of sp2 and sp3 islands are distinct features of an ideal carbon based matrix. Due to structural consistency and conjugated sp2 bonds in C-dots, conjugates exhibit good absorption and hence transfer the appropriate amount of energy to ionize DA. Fig. 2(b) shows the zeta potential of C-dots and its conjugate with DA. C-dots were synthesized from Citric acid, which has a carboxyl functional group with an overall negative zeta potential of −19.5mV. Additionally, once this carboxylic group is functionalized with cystamine hydrochloride, its zeta potential increases to −5.7mV, due to presence of NH3 + group. On further modification of C-dots with DA, the overall zeta potential increases to 17.9mV due to positively charged NH3 + group on DA. We recorded the FTIR measurements for understanding the attachment of the functional groups (Fig. 2(c)): the multiple broad bands from 1050 to 1280cm−1 are from CO and COC stretch vibrations. A sharp peak at 1350–1600cm−1 is approaching from the bending of CH bonds of alkene and also CC stretch. IR bands at 1740cm−1 (CO vibration stretch) and broad peak at 3200–3500cm−1 (OH vibration stretch) points out the subsistence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of C-dots [30,31]. On attaching cystamine hydrochloride on the surface of C-dots, the strong band at 3005cm−1 indicated a thiol (SH) bonding between the cystamine hydrochloride and the amine NH, as there is a major change in the amine stretch of the C-dots. FTIR spectrum of the DA [32] synthesized with conjugate C-dots is shown in green color in the same figure. As per our earlier discussion on origin of optical properties of C-dots, these functional groups are present at the edges of C-dots in order to break the energy traps and make electron–hole pairs to contribute to the overall fluorescence properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility of C-dots–DA conjugates in Neuro 2A cells
ce:section.ce:para: For IC50, the DA and C-dots–DA was supplied to the Neuro 2A cells. The IC 50 for bare DA was found to be 4μgmL−1 and for C-dots–DA, it was much higher (12μgmL−1). As C-dots have remarkable permeability toward the cell membranes [34], the overall efficiency of C-dots–DA conjugate, as drug delivery vehicle was excellent.

C-dots–DA showed excellent drug loading efficiency, which was found to be 78.23%. In Fig. 4 under same physiological conditions and as a function of time, drug release profile is also shown for bare DA. From the graph, it is observed that initially, drug released more in 4–6h due to higher concentration (ΔG =-ve) after which, DA released slowly up to 16% from 6 to 24h by using Chitosan nanoparticles as drug delivery vehicle and drug release was found to be 18% [21], where as our proposed study has shown an improved drug release profile (38.23%). C-dots exhibited higher efficiency in drug release, which is attributed to their small size as well as porous nature. C-dots mediated drug delivery has shown great potential [15,31]. The exact drug release pattern or its kinetics was assessed by many statistical models (zero order, Primary order, Higuchi model, Hixon–Crowell, Baker–Lonsdale model, Wibull model, Hopenfen burg model, Sequential layer model). In our case, the C-dots–DA follows Hixon–Crowell model, which is usually followed by materials, whose diameter and surface area varies considerably, e.g. powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence microscopy of Neuro 2A cells with DA and C-dots–DA
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of C-dots and C-dots–DA conjugate with Neuro 2A cells is an important step by which we can understand the biocompatibility or toxicity of nanomaterial. To confirm this, cells were treated with C-dots and C-dots–DA conjugate for 12h with a dose of 3mgmL−1. Cells after treatment were observed under the Epifluorescence microscope [35,36] (ESPA FI40-NIB-100F, ESPA systems Co. Ltd., Taiwan, excitation filter BP 490; barrier filter O 515). Fig. 5 shows the cellular imaging and internalization study of Neuro 2A cells. Fig. 5(a) and (c) displays the bright field images and blue fluorescence microscopy images of Neuro 2A cells incubated with C-dot as control. Similarly, Fig. 5(b) and (d) showed blue fluorescence via internalization of C-dots–DA in cells. The shape and number of cells unchanged, which confirms the biocompatibility of nanomaterial delivery in biological system. The faint blue fluorescence image, however, was observed due to the background noise and non-specific binding to medium components.

After 12h of injecting DA and C-dots–DA at 20μgmL−1 dose in mice, the necrosis studies were performed for important organs such as brain, heart, liver, kidney and spleen. The organs were sliced using the Microtomy instrument. Further, the tissues were stained with Haemotoxolin and Eosin dye. The tissue sections were observed by microscope (Fig. 6 ) and did not show any lesion or abnormality in histological tissue section of vital organs of mice [37]. It can be inferred by examining tissue sections of organs that injecting PBS (control), DA and C-dots–DA did not show any toxic effects on them.

No matter how effective and efficient a nanomaterial is in treating the diseases, unless it has been tested and confirmed for no side effects, the nanomaterial cannot be used successfully in delivering the drug cargo. For this reason, we must test our approach for body weight analysis. We injected 20μgmL−1 of dose in mice and measured their body weight till 45 days. We did not found any decrease in body weight or death in treated groups during our experiments, which can be seen in Fig. 7

C-dots–DA conjugates exhibited ideal drug release profile under physiological conditions. The drug release can be stable even over 60h. The newly designed dopamine nano-conjugate was found to be effective on the Neuro 2A cells as well as on the experimental mice models. Due to extremely small size of the C-dots, they can easily diffuse into blood capillaries thus contributing to the enhanced permeability of the drug. This novel nano-conjugate also showed no toxic effect on the weight of mice till 45 days of observation and the microtomy of vital organs did not showed any abnormality or lesion. We believe that the current approach can be used widely in the future drug delivery systems on the treatment of neuro diseases.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan. We also appreciate Bo-Sgum Wu (Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University) for the acquisition of chemicals and materials for this study.
